sourceMovie.title: Cake
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Cake (2014) stuck because it commits to its drama premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Daniel Barnz's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The film leans hard into its core genre, which is exactly what fans say they wanted more of from blockbusters in that era.

recommendations.title: Bird Box
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Bird Box (2018) belongs to the same conversation as Cake — both anchor their drama in horror emotion that doesn't let go. The dark undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Romeo + Juliet
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Cake's drama stayed with you, Romeo + Juliet (1996) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The romance through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: The Lovely Bones
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Cake excels in drama, The Lovely Bones (2009) doubles down on fantasy to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Antichrist
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Antichrist (2009) is the natural next step after Cake: same drama DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and horror elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Punch-Drunk Love
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Cake who respond to its drama will find Punch-Drunk Love (2002) immediately familiar. Both films share a romance sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Fathers and Daughters
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Cake fans keep landing on Fathers and Daughters (2015) — the drama is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Untraceable
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Untraceable draws from the same drama well as Cake, channeling thriller in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2008 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Cat on a Hot Tin Roof
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Cat on a Hot Tin Roof (1958) laid groundwork for the tone and themes that Cake later expanded. If Cake resonated for you, this predecessor shows where that emotional DNA began.

recommendations.title: Charlie Bartlett
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The drama appeal of Cake — that funny edge, the comedy — translates cleanly into Charlie Bartlett (2008). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Rabbit Hole
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Rabbit Hole (2010) clearly understood what makes drama work — the same quality that keeps Cake rewatchable. Expect the same tense rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

faq.question: What should I watch after Cake?
faq.answer: Try Bird Box, Romeo + Juliet, The Lovely Bones—each shares DNA with Cake while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Cake?
faq.answer: Cake (2014) is best described as Drama. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Cake worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 6.3/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this drama lane.
